title: DNS Provider Comparison
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes`, ThousandEyes OTel Tests Stream вАФ Metrics (DNS tests)
spl:
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="dns-server"
| stats avg(dns.lookup.duration) as avg_duration_s avg(dns.lookup.availability) as avg_availability by server.address, dns.question.name
| eval avg_duration_ms=round(avg_duration_s*1000,1)
| sort dns.question.name, avg_duration_ms
description: Comparing resolution times across DNS providers (internal recursive resolvers, external providers like Cloudflare, Google, ISP resolvers) helps optimize DNS configuration for lowest latency.
value: Comparing resolution times across DNS providers (internal recursive resolvers, external providers like Cloudflare, Google, ISP resolvers) helps optimize DNS configuration for lowest latency.
implementation: Create DNS Server tests in ThousandEyes for the same domain against multiple DNS server addresses. Each test targets a different resolver. Compare `dns.lookup.duration` and `dns.lookup.availability` across server addresses.
visualization: Column chart (resolution time by provider), Table (provider, domain, duration, availability), Comparison dashboard.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
вАҐ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk` (Splunkbase 7719).
вАҐ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=thousandeyes`, ThousandEyes OTel Tests Stream вАФ Metrics (DNS tests).
вАҐ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 вАФ Configure data collection
Create DNS Server tests in ThousandEyes for the same domain against multiple DNS server addresses. Each test targets a different resolver. Compare `dns.lookup.duration` and `dns.lookup.availability` across server addresses.

Step 2 вАФ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="dns-server"
| stats avg(dns.lookup.duration) as avg_duration_s avg(dns.lookup.availability) as avg_availability by server.address, dns.question.name
| eval avg_duration_ms=round(avg_duration_s*1000,1)
| sort dns.question.name, avg_duration_ms
```

Understanding this SPL

**DNS Provider Comparison** вАФ Comparing resolution times across DNS providers (internal recursive resolvers, external providers like Cloudflare, Google, ISP resolvers) helps optimize DNS configuration for lowest latency.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=thousandeyes`, ThousandEyes OTel Tests Stream вАФ Metrics (DNS tests). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk` (Splunkbase 7719). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedвАФrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

вАҐ Invokes macro `stream_index` вАФ in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
вАҐ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by server.address, dns.question.name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
вАҐ `eval` defines or adjusts **avg_duration_ms** вАФ often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
вАҐ Orders rows with `sort` вАФ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 вАФ Validate
Compare the same tests and time window in the Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk dashboard or the test view at app.thousandeyes.com so SplunkвАЩs metrics and states match the vendor. If they disagree, check streaming or HEC inputs, macros, and API or token health before retuning.

Step 4 вАФ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Column chart (resolution time by provider), Table (provider, domain, duration, availability), Comparison dashboard.
grandmaExplanation: We line up resolvers side by side, so we can tell if one public DNS path is off while another still works fine.
knownFalsePositives: Resolver comparisons jump when a provider throttles, moves anycast, or when tests hit different public resolvers.
